findings.description: The PDF is a single-page map/figure with data callouts positioned spatially around a map. The HTML converts this into a long vertically-scrolling page with a small map image at top followed by sequential tables, completely losing the spatial layout of the original.
findings.evidence: PDF: Data boxes (SD-1 through SD-5) are positioned around the map at specific geographic locations. HTML: All sample data is rendered as sequential tables below the map image.

findings.description: The SD-1/DUP-6102025-SD table in the PDF shows data inline within a callout box on the map. In the HTML it is rendered as a standalone table, which is functionally acceptable, but the 'J' qualifier flags on Benzo(a)anthracene (1.70 J) and Benzo(a)pyrene (0.868 J) are preserved, so data integrity is maintained. However, SD-2 in the PDF has columns for Arsenic, Cobalt, and Manganese only, while the HTML correctly reflects this. Risk Assessment values for SD-1 show CR and HI separately which is correctly replicated.
findings.evidence: Tables contain the same data values in both PDF and HTML, but spatial positioning context is lost.

findings.description: The map image in the HTML is significantly reduced in size and detail compared to the PDF. The PDF map is the primary content of the page showing detailed streets, parcels, sample locations, and stream paths. The HTML shows a small thumbnail-sized version that loses legibility of map features.
findings.evidence: PDF: Full-page detailed map with readable labels, roads, property boundaries. HTML: Small embedded image where text labels and geographic features are difficult to read.

findings.description: The title block information (SOUTHSIDE PARK LANDFILL, STREAM SEDIMENT ASSESSMENT) appears rotated vertically on the right side of the PDF but is rendered as horizontal header text in the HTML.
findings.evidence: PDF: Title block rotated 90 degrees on right margin. HTML: Title rendered horizontally at top of page.

findings.description: Reading order differs significantly. The PDF presents the map as the central element with data callouts arranged spatially. The HTML presents: title → map image → legend → SD-1 table → SD-2 table → SD-3 table → SD-4 table → SD-5 table → notes → references. While this is a reasonable linearization, a user loses the geographic context of which sample relates to which location.
findings.evidence: PDF: Reader sees map with data at specific geographic positions. HTML: Reader sees data in sequential order (SD-1 through SD-5) without spatial relationship to map locations.

findings.description: Heading hierarchy skips from h1 to h3 without an intervening h2 in the main content sections. The h2 is used for 'SOUTHSIDE PARK LANDFILL' in the header, but the sample data sections, notes, and references use h3 directly under the main element without a parent h2.
findings.evidence: HTML has h1 'STREAM SEDIMENT ASSESSMENT', h2 'SOUTHSIDE PARK LANDFILL' in header, then h3 'Legend', h3 'Sample Results', h3 'NOTES:', h3 'REFERENCE:' in the main body without a grouping h2.

findings.description: The HTML uses 'STREAM SEDIMENT ASSESSMENT' as the h1 document title, while in the PDF this appears as a sidebar/title block label. The actual document title could be considered 'SOUTHSIDE PARK LANDFILL'. This is a subjective structural choice and is acceptable as an AI conversion decision.
findings.evidence: PDF title block shows both 'STREAM SEDIMENT ASSESSMENT' and 'SOUTHSIDE PARK LANDFILL' as title elements.

findings.description: The HTML adds section headings 'Legend' and 'Sample Results' that do not appear in the source PDF. These are organizational headings added by the converter.
findings.evidence: PDF has no 'Legend' or 'Sample Results' headings; these are inferred structural labels added in HTML.

findings.description: Risk Assessment rows are included within the sample data tables as regular table rows rather than being structurally separated. While all data values are correct, the 'Risk Assessment' label appears as a bold cell in the first column with an empty second column, which is a minor structural difference from the PDF's callout box formatting.
findings.evidence: PDF shows Risk Assessment as a sub-section within each callout box; HTML embeds it as table rows with bold formatting.

findings.description: The HTML has a nested <main> element inside another <main> element, which is an HTML structural issue but does not cause content duplication.
findings.evidence: The outer <body> contains <main> from the template, and inside it another <main> element is opened for the content sections.
